ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to okonomiyaki exists; mapped to broader pancake food product class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific okonomiyaki code; A0CKM (pancakes and similar batter-based products) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for okonomiyaki or Japanese savoury pancake; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of okonomiyaki is one whole pancake weighing approximately 200 g, as commonly served in Japanese restaurants and street-food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Okonomiyaki — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Okonomiyaki (cr_id F05010006). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference for comparable Japanese savoury pancake (USDA SR Legacy item 'Pancakes, plain, prepared from recipe') as HPB does not report saturated fat separately; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for okonomiyaki was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the closest analogous food: wheat-flour pancake with mixed fillings, which has a reported GI of approximately 52–58 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The wheat-flour batter base, moderate fibre from cabbage (2.7 g/100 g), and protein from egg and seafood collectively moderate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (200 g × 12.6 g/100 g = 25.2 g) / 100 = 13.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 451 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit okonomiyaki portion size to no more than 100 g and request reduced okonomiyaki sauce and mayonnaise, as these condiments are the primary sodium contributors alongside dashi stock; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 902 mg sodium, representing 39% of the 2300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (12.6 g/100 g) with 2.7 g dietary fibre and estimated low GI of 55
type2_diabetes.guidance: Okonomiyaki is acceptable in moderate portions for people with type 2 diabetes due to its low GI (~55) and meaningful fibre content from cabbage; a standard 200 g serving provides approximately 25 g carbohydrate and should be counted within the meal carbohydrate budget, with okonomiyaki sauce minimised to reduce added sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (451 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (7.79 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~1.9 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, okonomiyaki should be consumed occasionally and in small portions; the high sodium content is the primary cardiovascular concern, while the moderate total fat and low estimated saturated fat (~1.9 g/100 g) are less problematic, and the seafood filling variant is preferred over pork belly to reduce saturated fat and arachidonic acid load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory cabbage and omega-3 from bonito/aonori offset by high sodium and pork belly variant risk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The seafood-filled variant of okonomiyaki is preferable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to omega-3 contribution from bonito flakes and anti-inflammatory sulforaphane from cabbage; the pork belly variant should be avoided as it increases arachidonic acid (omega-6) intake, and sauce should be minimised to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Okonomiyaki (お好み焼き) is a Japanese savoury pancake originating from the Kansai and Hiroshima regions of Japan. The name translates literally as 'grilled as you like it', reflecting the dish's customisable nature. It is made from a wheat-flour batter mixed with shredded cabbage, egg, dashi stock, and a protein filling — most commonly seafood (squid, shrimp, scallops) or pork belly — then cooked on a flat iron griddle (teppan). The finished pancake is topped with okonomiyaki sauce (a thick, sweet-savoury condiment), Japanese mayonnaise, aonori (dried green seaweed flakes), and katsuobushi (dried bonito flakes that wave in the heat). The texture is soft and slightly chewy inside with a lightly crisped exterior. Per 100 g, okonomiyaki provides 146 kcal, 6.3 g protein, 7.79 g fat, 12.6 g carbohydrate, 2.7 g dietary fibre, and 451 mg sodium. The high sodium content (451 mg/100 g) makes it unsuitable for hypertension without sauce reduction. The moderate GI (~55) and fibre from cabbage make it acceptable in controlled portions for type 2 diabetes management. The dish is a common working-lunch and snack item in Japanese cuisine and is widely available in Japanese restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Can I eat okonomiyaki if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05010006) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous pancake entry. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous wheat-flour pancake data. All mandatory Japanese-language naming satisfied.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.9 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain pancake prepared from recipe (NDB 18290) as the HPB Foodo record does not report saturated fat separately; this is a reasonable proxy given the similar wheat-flour-egg-fat composition. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for okonomiyaki (F05010006) and are not available in a directly matching approved-source record as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour pancake with mixed fillings from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct okonomiyaki GI measurement was found in any approved source. A GI of 55 is classified as 'low'. 4. The dietary_tag 'low_calorie' from the input cultural section is retained as contextual metadata; at 146 kcal/100 g this is plausible for a mixed savoury pancake. 5. Halal status is marked false as okonomiyaki typically contains pork belly in the standard preparation; seafood variants may be halal-certifiable but the generic record cannot confirm this.

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 55, as per University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI 55 or less is low). Updated relevant health_context and natural_language_description accordingly.
